text: We use a term that we call nail it then
scale it. All right? And it's really
nail it in order to scale it. And the
problem is most people learn how to

little bit and then think, "Oh, I just
need to do more of that." But here's the
problem with that. Until you find out
what the compounding vehicle is within
your business, you're bound to plateau.
And so I've done this example before,
but I think it's so powerful that it's
worth repeating. All right? So let's say
that you have company A and company B.
All right? And both of them sell 100

per year. B sells 100 the first year,
200 the second year, 300 the third year.
Well, which company would you rather
have at face value? You'd think, "Oh,
I'd rather have B." But wait, there's
more. Let's say company B loses 100

that good at what they do or they don't

means that next year they lose 200 and
next year lose 300. Now let's say
company A keeps 100% of their customers.
So year two they actually have 200
customers and year three they have 300
customers. They have the original 200
plus this 100. So now it's 300 in total.
So both of these look like they're 300

these would you now rather have? This
guy. Because the margins are going to be
better because you don't have to pay to

They had to pay to acquire 600

This one only had to pay to acquire 300.

this is a business that literally will
